story:
It’s her. It has to be. I never felt this until after she died. Then again, I’ve never been bereaved before, so who knows? The irony wouldn’t be lost on Lucy—wouldn’t or isn’t, I’m still not sure—which is exactly the kind of modal-verb nitpick I’d throw at her while she rolled her eyes and called me a prick.

I’m the crossword-and-Wordle type; she was kinetic, a creature of cliffs and gorges. If there’s an afterlife, of course she’d find a physical way to reach me. And this touch isn’t wind. Not the forecast kind that bends trees. It’s still out here—Indian-summer still—until that soft, all-at-once brush along my bare arms and legs in places that take your breath away. Villages and museums? Nothing. Limestone walls and a sky slashed with light? There she is.

Or maybe it’s just a rogue puff and a grieving brain. But if it is her, she’s provoking me. We left so much unfinished.

In New Zealand we maxed out our cards and nearly each other. I’d made my case: I like knowing I won’t drown in level-five rapids. She countered with “You’re only truly alive when you’re about to die,” and I countered with the Dalai Lama. She wanted the skydive; I didn’t. “If you don’t get this, you don’t get me,” she said, voice like granite. I let her jump. She hugged me afterward, then shoved me off: “You’re a lightweight.” “No,” I said. “Just different.”

We patched it, briefly. Then kidney cancer showed up late. She’d masked the symptoms with motion. No coaster, no canyon could outrun it. I tried anyway. On a two-hundred-foot drop I screamed; she raised her hands and whooped like someone who didn’t have long. Later she dragged me to karaoke. “No one really dies,” she said. “Either you loop back to learn or you graduate to bliss. So grab it.” She butchered Whitney joyfully; I murdered Freddie meekly. The silence on the ride home said everything.

Halfway back she grabbed my belt loop. “Next left.” The gorge. Night, clear, lamplit path. Her idea: tick off the most obvious bucket-list cliché. I wanted to but couldn’t—not twenty metres from a footpath. Panic. She begged; I froze. “It’s not brain science.” “Surgery,” I snapped, petty and small. She erupted. We never got intimate again. She slipped away soon after. Every day since, I’ve asked whether she still loved me at the end.

So now I’m on the bridge over the gorge, a few hundred metres from where we failed under the oaks and birches. Sparrows circle. The sun cuts the grey. The caress returns. Is it her? Is she urging me across? Has she graduated to eternal whatever, or am I about to plummet into rocks and a punchline? Or is it just wind?

I remember the first day: green eyes, pale auburn hair, the smudged rockstar liner that looked better than perfect. We loved our differences until we didn’t. Without her, my life feels like nothing. The breeze presses: do what she’d do.

And then I understand. For it to mean anything, the risk has to be mine. Not borrowed bravado, not a shortcut to where she might be, but the daily terror of living wide open without her—saying yes, singing badly, holding my nerve in full view of strangers. To honour her, I have to choose life’s edge, not its end.

Three. Two. One… I step away.
